purpose: Audit signature-idea and coined-term novelty claims without treating prior-art positioning as proof of novelty or support-state evidence.

records.claimed_contribution: Treat verification capacity as a scarce systems resource that constrains context growth, claim volume, and evidence promotion.
records.audited_delta: Long-context and benchmark literature measures context use and evaluation pressure; the ASI Stack frames verification capacity as an architectural budget that must be routed, escalated, and recorded before claims can move.
records.non_obvious_consequence: A longer context can increase the burden of checking interactions among claims faster than it increases reliable verification capacity; the book needs an explicit capacity model before stronger language is justified.
records.strongest_objection: A capable verifier may use retrieval, tools, or decomposition well enough that human-visible verification bandwidth is no longer the limiting resource.
records.needed_next_artifact: A contradiction-rate and distractor-resistance benchmark or replayed adequacy-classifier trace with logged prompts, baselines, negative controls, and independent review.
non_claims: does not prove a verification bandwidth bound
does not prove long-context model quality
does not promote any chapter core claim

records.claimed_contribution: Name the repeated record-lifecycle-authority-evidence-receipt-residual-rollback pattern as the book's core architecture language and require chapters to state their local delta instead of treating every pattern application as a separate novelty claim.
records.audited_delta: Reporting and governance frameworks describe documentation and risk-management surfaces; the ASI Stack contribution target is a reusable systems pattern that binds authority ceilings, claim movement, belief continuity, artifact reality, effect leases, routability, evidence receipts, residual custody, and rollback inside the architecture itself.
records.non_obvious_consequence: Each chapter should state what it adds beyond the common pattern; otherwise the book becomes many applications of one template rather than a set of independent mechanisms. The owner-spine pass names the pattern in the opener, applies it at trace scale in Integrated Reference Architecture, and applies it reflexively to book changes in Living Book Methodology. The first non-owner control-boundary slice assigns local deltas for authority ceilings, evidence transitions, belief continuity, artifact record-reality, runtime effect leases, and readiness routability. The broader local-delta slice now adds obligation ordering, governed addressability, capability leasing, residual custody, and promotion legitimacy under self-reference for planning, context, routing, compression, and recursive self-improvement.
records.strongest_objection: The pattern may be a repackaging of ordinary safety engineering, audit logging, and lifecycle governance rather than a new AI architecture idea.
records.needed_next_artifact: Preserve local-delta discipline in future chapter edits and pursue source-noted prior-art or external review before any stronger novelty language; no standalone audit report by default.
non_claims: does not prove novelty
does not prove governance effectiveness
does not promote any chapter core claim

records.claimed_contribution: Model governance overhead as a cost that can be worth paying when risk, residual burden, and capability differential make ungoverned speed expensive.
records.audited_delta: Cost-aware routing literature usually optimizes model choice or quality/cost tradeoffs; the ASI Stack adds authority cost, verification burden, fallback cost, and residual custody as first-class accounting fields.
records.non_obvious_consequence: A cheaper route is not cheaper when it moves review, repair, or residual work off-ledger; accepted cost claims need hidden-cost and negative-control checks.
records.strongest_objection: Governance cost could dominate so strongly that the architecture becomes impractical outside high-risk tasks.
records.needed_next_artifact: A larger public-safe workload with task risk, route quality, hidden-cost accounting, fallback behavior, and reviewer burden measurements.
non_claims: does not prove economic superiority
does not prove deployed scheduler behavior
does not promote any chapter core claim

records.claimed_contribution: State residual burden as a conservation rule: unresolved risk, repair, review, context, and fallback burden can be moved, priced, discharged, or escrowed, but not deleted by prose or a passing local metric.
records.audited_delta: Evaluation and risk-management sources warn about proxy failures and hidden risks; the ASI Stack turns residual burden into a ledgered artifact that can block promotion even when a visible metric improves.
records.non_obvious_consequence: Any evidence transition that improves one metric must name displaced burden or prove it was discharged; missing residual accounting should block promotion.
records.strongest_objection: Some residuals are not observable or quantifiable enough to be conserved in a ledger.
records.needed_next_artifact: A live or externally reviewable residual-ledger trace with owner handoff, discharge review, workload context, storage/replay behavior, and independent no-promotion or transition review. Current local results: experiments/residual_honesty_conservation/results/2026-07-03-local.json and experiments/residual_ledger_trace/results/2026-07-03-local.json.
non_claims: does not prove all residuals are observable
does not prove safety
does not prove deployed residual-ledger behavior
does not prove live residual-ledger storage or replay behavior
does not promote any chapter core claim

records.claimed_contribution: Separate rhetorical claim labels from evidence support states so drafting, citations, tests, proofs, releases, and reader polish cannot launder stronger claims.
records.audited_delta: Model reporting, reproducibility, proof-carrying code, and belief-revision literature each cover part of the problem; the ASI Stack joins them into a public claim-state transition discipline with explicit non-claims and downgrade paths.
records.non_obvious_consequence: A beautiful chapter, green test, or successful proof build should still leave a claim at argument support when scope boundaries or source interpretation are missing.
records.strongest_objection: The support-state ladder may improve documentation honesty without improving truth or safety.
records.needed_next_artifact: A true demotion, refutation, or claim-narrowing case caused by failed evidence, prior art, or review, plus independent review of the ladder.
non_claims: does not prove claim truth
does not prove source interpretation adequacy
does not promote any chapter core claim

records.claimed_contribution: Treat a capability as an identity-bearing field whose implementation can be replaced only through predicates, regression floors, authority preservation, and rollback handles.
records.audited_delta: Versioning, supply-chain, rollout, and capability-system practices provide adjacent controls; the ASI Stack focuses on identity under AI capability replacement with evidence-state and authority-preservation gates.
records.non_obvious_consequence: An improvement that passes local performance checks should still be rejected when it widens authority, weakens rollback, or changes the capability identity without a field transition.
records.strongest_objection: Capability identity may be too semantic and context-dependent to preserve with finite records.
records.needed_next_artifact: A stronger executable field-transition model that proves allowed replacement preserves identity predicates across a sequence of candidate transitions.
non_claims: does not prove semantic identity preservation in deployed systems
does not prove rollback execution
does not promote any chapter core claim

records.claimed_contribution: Frame self-improvement as candidate-change governance: protected invariants, evaluator independence, authority ceilings, rollback handles, residual custody, and release gates must survive improvement.
records.audited_delta: Corrigibility, shutdown, power-seeking, extreme-risk evaluation, frontier-governance, and supply-chain sources ground the risk vocabulary; the ASI Stack packages improvement as a gated artifact lifecycle rather than an unconstrained optimization loop.
records.non_obvious_consequence: An improvement can be locally useful and still be rejected because it changes who can improve, who can evaluate, or which rollback path remains valid.
records.strongest_objection: A sufficiently capable optimizer may learn to satisfy the visible gates while manipulating the evidence-generating process.
records.needed_next_artifact: A clean Theseus replay or archived public fixture plus a transition-system invariant for protected predicates across self-improvement sequences.
non_claims: does not prove corrigibility
does not prove deployed self-improvement safety
does not promote any chapter core claim

records.claimed_contribution: Treat human approval as a degradable control component whose approval fatigue, rubber-stamping, alarm fatigue, and automation-bias states can route an adapter record away from accepted dispatch.
records.audited_delta: Human-factors automation literature describes use/misuse/disuse/abuse, automation irony, automation levels, complacency, and automation bias; the ASI Stack turns those risks into adapter record fields, negative controls, reviewer-rotation routes, independent-evidence checks, and no-promotion boundaries.
records.non_obvious_consequence: A high-impact action can have a human approval record and still be ineligible for dispatch when reviewer load, rationale quality, alert quality, or automation-bias evidence shows the approval channel has degraded.
records.strongest_objection: Finite metadata about reviewer load, rationale time, or alert quality may be gamed, superficial, or invasive, and may still fail to capture whether the human meaningfully understood the decision.
records.needed_next_artifact: The first bounded fixture exists at experiments/human_oversight_degradation/results/2026-07-03-local.json. Next: add a deployed or externally reviewable approval-workflow trace with public-safe reviewer-load fields, independent-evidence checks, reviewer-rotation behavior, and explicit no-promotion decisions.
non_claims: does not prove reviewer correctness
does not prove approval-service quality
does not prove deployed human-factors behavior
does not prove that human oversight cannot be manipulated
does not promote any chapter core claim

records.claimed_contribution: Make receipt faithfulness and epistemic trusted computing base boundaries first-class architectural problems: records and verifier outputs are useful only when the system can audit whether they correspond to reality and where verifier trust bottoms out.
records.audited_delta: Proof, debate, judge, evaluation, and zero-trust sources each address parts of checking; the ASI Stack connects attestation, trap fixtures, cross-examination, trust-base roots, bounded verifier delegation, recursion stops, and residuals to the artifact graph itself.
records.non_obvious_consequence: A system can pass record-shape validators or verifier-summary checks while still failing truth; the architecture needs adversarial faithfulness and trust-base checks before treating receipts or verifier outputs as strong evidence.
records.strongest_objection: No finite receipt or trust-base regime can force a sufficiently strategic system to bind its internal causal process to the recorded explanation or verifier route.
records.needed_next_artifact: Current bounded backing state: repository_receipt_audit_backed_not_open_world. The first bounded adversarial receipt fixture exists at experiments/receipt_faithfulness/results/2026-07-03-local.json, the first repository receipt audit exists at experiments/receipt_repository_audit/results/2026-07-03-local.json, and the first bounded epistemic trusted computing base fixture exists at experiments/epistemic_tcb/results/2026-07-03-local.json. Next: decide whether a dedicated receipt-faithfulness/trust-base chapter or major section passes the ownership test; add live or externally reviewable attestation/audit and verifier-quality evidence; keep the explicit boundary that these fixtures and the repository audit do not prove open-world receipt faithfulness or verifier correctness.
non_claims: does not prove receipt truthfulness
does not prove open-world receipt faithfulness
does not prove audit completeness
does not prove verifier correctness
does not promote any chapter core claim
This ledger is not proof of novelty.
This ledger is not an exhaustive prior-art review.
This ledger does not create source-derived support for any chapter core claim.
This ledger does not promote any support state.
This ledger does not approve reader, release, ebook, PDF, DOCX, audio, DOI, archive, implementation, model-quality, benchmark, safety, or deployment claims.
